common_name: Mexican Zinnia (Little Classic Zinnia, Haage Zinnia)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mexican zinnia is more drought-tolerant, heat-tolerant, and disease-resistant than common garden zinnia (Z. elegans) and is outstanding for hot, humid Gulf Coast summers where powdery mildew can devastate Z. elegans plantings.

directions: Direct sow seeds in warm soil in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; thin to 8–12 inches. Zinnia haageana (Mexican zinnia) is a compact, fine-leaved, heat-tolerant Mexican native zinnia species that produces cheerful bicolor and tricolor daisy flowers in orange, red, yellow, and mahogany combinations from summer through frost. Unlike the common large-flowered garden zinnia (Z. elegans), Z. haageana is dramatically more drought-tolerant, heat-tolerant, and resistant to powdery mildew — the disease that devastates Z. elegans in hot, humid climates. In zone 9, Mexican zinnias perform with vigor through the summer while common garden zinnias may defoliate from mildew. The 'Aztec' series is the most widely grown, with single or semi-double flowers in warm orange-red, yellow-orange, and burnt-orange combinations that are extremely attractive to butterflies, especially Gulf Fritillaries and Sulphurs. The compact habit and narrow, lance-shaped leaves create a finer texture than Z. elegans. Z. haageana is also used as a parent in interspecific zinnia hybrids ('Profusion,' 'Zahara') bred specifically for heat, humidity, and disease resistance.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Annual Flowers

common_name: Strawberry Fields Gomphrena (Haage Globe Amaranth, Wild Globe Amaranth)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; soak seeds for 12–24 hours before sowing; plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Gomphrena haageana is slightly more heat-tolerant and larger-growing than common globe amaranth (G. globosa) and produces superior long-stemmed cut flowers.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting (soaking seeds first improves germination); plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Gomphrena haageana (Haage globe amaranth) produces clover-like, papery, rounded flower heads 1–1.5 inches across in vivid orange-red to scarlet (the 'Strawberry Fields' cultivar) or golden-orange, on taller, more upright stems than common globe amaranth (G. globosa). The papery flower heads maintain their color perfectly when dried — making them among the best everlasting flowers for dried arrangements, where the orange-red 'Strawberry Fields' color is one of the most sought-after and unique in the dried flower palette. Heat and drought tolerance is exceptional — plants bloom prolifically through Gulf Coast summers without mildew or decline. The flowers are also outstanding for fresh cutting and as a long-season garden annual. Butterflies visit the rounded flower heads frequently. 'Strawberry Fields' (vivid red-orange) is the most widely grown cultivar; 'QIS Orange' is selected for commercial cut-flower production.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flowers

common_name: Vinca (Rosy Periwinkle, Annual Vinca, Madagascar Periwinkle)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting (slow-growing from seed); or purchase bedding plants; in zone 9, plant after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; annual vinca is among the most heat-tolerant summer annuals and thrives through Gulf Coast summers where many other annuals struggle.

directions: Plant bedding plants in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; avoid overwatering or heavy mulch against stems, which promotes Phytophthora stem and root rot. Catharanthus roseus (annual vinca or Madagascar periwinkle) is one of the top summer annuals for hot, humid Gulf Coast and Deep South gardens — an exceptionally heat-tolerant, long-blooming Madagascar native producing 1.5-to-2-inch, 5-petaled, pinwheel-shaped flowers in red, pink, rose, magenta, white, lavender, and bicolor (with contrasting eye) in profusion from spring through frost, with shiny, dark-green, camellia-like leaves. Unlike impatiens (which struggles or dies in humid summers due to downy mildew), annual vinca thrives through the hottest, most humid months. It is also valuable medically — Catharanthus roseus is the source of the alkaloids vincristine and vinblastine, critical chemotherapy drugs used worldwide for leukemia and lymphoma treatment. The 'Titan,' 'Cora,' and 'Pacifica' series are modern, improved cultivars with exceptional heat tolerance and disease resistance to aerial Phytophthora.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flowers
North Carolina State Extension — Summer Annuals

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Bougainvillea glabra blooms most heavily under mild drought stress (slightly dry) and full sun — avoid rich, wet soil or excessive fertilization, which promotes vegetative growth at the expense of the showy bracts.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; provide a sturdy trellis, wall, or fence for climbing support. Bougainvillea glabra (smooth bougainvillea) is the species most commonly sold as 'bougainvillea' in the nursery trade — a vigorous, thorny, sprawling vine or shrub from Brazil producing masses of small, white, tubular true flowers surrounded by the magnificently colored bracts (modified leaves) in magenta, purple, red, orange, yellow, white, or bicolor combinations that are the ornamental feature of the plant. B. glabra differs from the common hybrid Bougainvillea spectabilis in having smooth (glabrous, as the name implies) stems and leaves vs. hairy stems and is generally considered more free-blooming and compact. Bougainvillea blooms most heavily after mild water stress in full sun; withhold water before anticipated bloom periods to trigger flowering. In zone 9, it may die back in hard freezes but resprouts vigorously from roots when established; in zones 10–12, it is a dramatic year-round bloomer. Stems are armed with sharp thorns; handle with heavy gloves.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bougainvillea

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; Jatropha integerrima is one of the best year-round blooming shrubs for South Florida and zone 10 gardens and performs well as a warm-season annual or container plant in zone 9.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Jatropha is drought-tolerant once established and blooms most heavily in full sun. CAUTION: All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested — keep away from children and pets. Jatropha integerrima (spicy jatropha or peregrina) is a Cuban native tropical shrub producing dense, rounded clusters of brilliant scarlet-red, star-shaped flowers 1 inch across almost continuously throughout the warm months in zones 10–12, and reliably spring through fall in zone 9. The flowers are irresistible to butterflies (especially Sulphurs, Zebra Longwings, and Gulf Fritillaries) and hummingbirds, making it an outstanding wildlife plant for tropical gardens. The attractive, slightly lobed, dark-green to bronze-tinged leaves add ornamental interest between bloom flushes. Despite the toxicity of its seeds and sap (all Jatropha species contain toxic diterpenes), the plant is widely grown as a landscape shrub in South Florida, the Caribbean, and tropical regions; wear gloves when pruning. It grows rapidly from cuttings and can be trained as a small tree for greater impact.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Shrubs
